notes: Final batch, completing the full GeoLogos location set alongside batches 1-4. Same format: 4 event-based clues per location, ordered hardest -> easiest, plus modernCountry, ancientRegion, and a didYouKnow fact.

clues: Jesus withdrew to this region and encountered a persistent Canaanite woman seeking healing for her daughter.
Hiram, king of this city, supplied cedar and skilled workers to help build Solomon's Temple.
Ezekiel pronounced a famous prophecy of judgment against this wealthy trading city.
It was a major Phoenician port city on the Mediterranean coast, north of Israel.
locations.didYouKnow: Tyre was so confident in its island fortress that Alexander the Great famously had to build an entire land bridge out to sea just to conquer it.

clues: Jesus's ministry and reputation reached crowds coming from this coastal city.
It's frequently paired with its sister city, Tyre, as a symbol of pagan wealth and pride in prophetic warnings.
Jezebel, the infamous queen who promoted Baal worship in Israel, came from this city's royal family.
It sat on the Phoenician coast, north of Israel, near Tyre.
locations.didYouKnow: Sidon is considered one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world, mentioned in Genesis as one of Canaan's sons.

clues: Elijah was sent here during a severe famine, to the house of a poor foreign widow.
Her jar of flour and jug of oil miraculously never ran out for the rest of the famine.
Elijah later raised her son back to life after the boy stopped breathing.
It sat on the Phoenician coast between Tyre and Sidon.
locations.didYouKnow: Jesus specifically referenced this widow centuries later, noting that Elijah was sent to her rather than to any widow in Israel.

clues: This was one of the five major Philistine cities, home to some of their most feared warriors.
Goliath, the giant who challenged Israel's army, came from this city.
David briefly sought refuge here from Saul, feigning madness to survive among his enemies.
It sat inland from the Mediterranean coast, in Philistine territory.
locations.didYouKnow: Gath is associated with unusually tall warriors in the Bible, likely explaining why it produced legendary giants like Goliath.

clues: The captured Ark of the Covenant was brought here as war plunder by the Philistines.
The statue of their god Dagon was found fallen face-down before the Ark, then found broken the next morning.
The people here were struck with tumors until they finally sent the Ark away.
It was one of the five major Philistine coastal cities.
locations.didYouKnow: The Philistines moved the Ark between multiple cities trying to escape the plagues it brought, before finally sending it back to Israel entirely.

clues: This was the last of the Philistine cities to receive the captured Ark of the Covenant before it was sent home.
King Ahaziah of Israel sent messengers here to consult its god about whether he would recover from an injury.
Elijah intercepted those messengers and rebuked the king for seeking a foreign god instead of the Lord.
It was one of the five major Philistine cities, the northernmost of them.
locations.didYouKnow: Ekron's god, Baal-zebub, whose name means 'lord of the flies,' later became associated with a name for the devil himself.

clues: A wealthy woman here regularly hosted the prophet Elisha and built him a small room to stay in.
Elisha promised her a son as a reward for her hospitality, even though she had none and her husband was old.
Years later, that same son died suddenly, and she rushed to find Elisha for help.
Elisha raised the boy back to life after stretching himself out over him.
locations.didYouKnow: This woman is never named in the text, remembered simply as 'the Shunammite woman' for her extraordinary hospitality and faith.

clues: This fishing town was the hometown of Peter, Andrew, and Philip.
Jesus healed a blind man here in stages, first seeing men 'like trees walking' before full sight was restored.
Jesus fed a crowd of thousands near this town with just a few loaves and fish.
It sat on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee, near Capernaum.
locations.didYouKnow: Despite Jesus performing many miracles here, he specifically pronounced a woe over this town for its lack of repentance.

clues: A wealthy and secret follower of Jesus came from this town.
That man, Joseph, boldly asked Pilate for Jesus's body after the crucifixion.
He wrapped Jesus in linen and laid him in his own new, unused tomb.
This act fulfilled a prophecy that the Messiah would be with a rich man in his death.
locations.didYouKnow: The exact modern location of Arimathea is still debated among scholars, though it's traditionally linked to a site northwest of Jerusalem.

clues: Jesus sent two disciples ahead into this village to find a young donkey tied there.
He instructed them that if anyone asked, they should simply say 'the Lord needs it.'
It served as the starting point for Jesus's triumphal entry into Jerusalem.
It sat near Bethany, on the Mount of Olives, just outside Jerusalem.
locations.didYouKnow: Bethphage's name means 'house of unripe figs,' likely describing the produce of the area.

clues: This was the hometown and burial place of the prophet Samuel.
David fled here to Samuel for protection when Saul was trying to kill him.
Its name was later invoked by the prophet Jeremiah to describe a mother weeping for her children.
That same image was later applied to the grief following the massacre of infants after Jesus's birth.
locations.didYouKnow: The phrase 'a voice was heard in Ramah, weeping' connects an Old Testament image of grief directly to the New Testament's account of Herod's slaughter of the innocents.

clues: Israel camped here just before crossing the Jordan River into the Promised Land.
A foreign prophet named Balaam was hired to curse Israel nearby but blessed them instead, despite his talking donkey's warnings.
The Israelites fell into idolatry and immorality with Moabite women while camped here, provoking a plague.
Joshua sent two spies out from here to scout Jericho before the conquest began.
locations.didYouKnow: Balaam's donkey is one of only two animals in the entire Bible recorded as speaking with a human voice, the other being the serpent in Eden.

clues: Samson carried off this city's heavy gates on his shoulders after being trapped inside overnight.
He was later captured, blinded, and imprisoned here after Delilah betrayed him.
He brought down a great temple here, killing more Philistines in his death than in his life.
It was one of the five major Philistine cities, the southernmost along the coast.
locations.didYouKnow: Gaza remains a real, densely inhabited coastal region in the Middle East today, carrying the same name it did in Samson's time.
